meta.title: Work, Gravitational Potential Energy & Kinetic Energy
meta.subject: Physics · Energy

examples.q: A force of 25 N pushes a box 4 m along the floor in the same direction as the force. How much work is done?
examples.solution:
Work is done when a force moves an object
through a displacement.

Formula:
    W = F × d × cos θ

where  W = work done     (J — Joules)
       F = applied force  (N)
       d = displacement   (m)
       θ = angle between force and displacement

When force and displacement are in the SAME direction,
θ = 0° and cos 0° = 1, so:
    W = F × d

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F = 25 N
    d = 4 m

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    W = 25 × 4
    W = 100 J

Answer:  W = 100 J

examples.q: A 3 kg book is placed on a shelf 2 m above the ground. What is its gravitational potential energy? (g = 10 m/s²)
examples.solution:
Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE) is the energy
stored in an object due to its height above
a reference point (usually the ground).

Formula:
    GPE = m × g × h

where  m = mass                   (kg)
       g = gravitational field strength  (10 m/s² on Earth)
       h = height above reference point  (m)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    m = 3 kg
    g = 10 m/s²
    h = 2 m

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    GPE = 3 × 10 × 2
    GPE = 60 J

Answer:  GPE = 60 J

examples.q: A 2 kg ball moves at 5 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
examples.solution:
Kinetic Energy (KE) is the energy an object has
because of its motion.

Formula:
    KE = ½ × m × v²

where  m = mass   (kg)
       v = speed  (m/s)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    m = 2 kg
    v = 5 m/s

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    KE = ½ × 2 × (5)²
    KE = ½ × 2 × 25
    KE = 25 J

Answer:  KE = 25 J

Note: KE depends on v² — doubling speed
quadruples kinetic energy.

questions.q: A 10 N force pushes a cart 5 m in the direction of the force. How much work is done?
questions.given: Formula:  W = F × d  (when force and displacement are parallel)

questions.q: A 4 kg object is lifted to a height of 3 m. What is its gravitational potential energy? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 5 kg object moves at 6 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
No work is done on an object when the force applied is perpendicular to displacement. Why?
questions.given:
Formula:  W = F × d × cos θ
Consider what happens when θ = 90°.
opts: cos 90° = 0, so W = 0 regardless of F or d
The object moves in a circle
questions.sol: W = F × d × cos 90° = F × d × 0 = 0 J. A perpendicular force (like normal force) does no work.

questions.q: A ball is dropped from a height of 5 m. What was its GPE just before dropping? (m = 2 kg, g = 10 m/s²)
If an object's speed doubles, what happens to its kinetic energy?
questions.given:
Formula:  KE = ½ × m × v²
Analyze the effect of doubling v.
questions.sol: KE = ½mv². If v doubles, v² becomes 4× larger. KE quadruples.

questions.q: A 3 kg object falls from rest at a height of 10 m. What is its KE just before hitting the ground? (g = 10 m/s²)
questions.given:
By conservation of energy:
    GPE lost = KE gained
    mgh = KE

questions.q: What is the unit of work and energy?
questions.given:
Work = Force × distance = N × m.
This unit has a special name in physics.
questions.sol: The Joule (J) is the unit of work and energy. 1 J = 1 N·m.

questions.q: A force of 20 N is applied at 60° to the direction of motion over 5 m. How much work is done?
questions.given:
Formula:  W = F × d × cos θ
cos 60° = 0.5

questions.q: An object has KE = 200 J and mass = 8 kg. What is its speed?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  KE = ½ × m × v²
→  v² = 2 × KE / m
→  v = √(2 × KE / m)
